aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The extraction is mostly accurate but contains a systematic error in evidence_type classification. Relationship assertions (child-of-parent inferences) are correctly marked as indirect, but non-relationship assertions for all five people—name, age, birthplace, occupation—are marked as 'direct' when they should be marked 'indirect'. On pre-1880 censuses without explicit relationship columns, the identities of household members reporting the information cannot be confirmed; the informant is unknown and could be anyone in the household. A stated fact from an unidentified informant should be classified as 'indirect' evidence of that fact, not 'direct'. Direct evidence means the fact is explicitly stated in a document; indirect means it is stated but the informant's reliability is secondhand or the source lacks explicit confirmation. The skill correctly noted informants are unknown, but incorrectly scored the evidence as direct.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted assertions for all five household members (Patrick, Mary, Thomas, Bridget, John) as required. For each person, it captured available fields: name, age, birthplace, residence, occupation (Patrick only—correct, as others had blank fields), and relationship assertions for the three children. No assertions were created for blank occupation fields. The skill also created appropriate couple and parent-child relationships in the tree. All required content was addressed.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All four MCP tool calls were executed correctly. The research_log_append call properly logged the user-provided record with ark, dwelling/family identifiers, and pertinent notes. The research_append call correctly structured all 27 assertions with proper record_id (ark), fact_type, values, and metadata, and created the source entry with correct citation format and date fields. The two tree_edit calls properly added five new persons and seven relationships (one couple, six parent-child pairs) with correct person IDs (I2–I6) and relationship types. No fixture_not_found errors occurred; all args matched expected patterns.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion represents a single extractable fact. Name, age, birthplace, occupation, and residence are separate assertions for each person. Relationship assertions are atomic: two separate assertions for each child (one relationship to Patrick, one to Mary). The skill did not compound facts—e.g., no assertion conflated age with birth year or mixed multiple facts into a single value field. The design correctly follows the schema's intention: date+place on one event assertion is not compound; separate distinct facts are decomposed into separate entries.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identified informants for most assertions but made one critical error on the relationship assertions. For name/age/birthplace/occupation facts, it properly specified 'unknown household member' with reasoned proximity ('likely self' for adults, 'likely a parent' for children) and proximity 'household_member'—the correct doctrine for pre-1880 census facts where the respondent is not recorded. The informant_bias_notes correctly note that the 1850 census does not identify the respondent. However, for relationship assertions (child-of-parent inferences), the skill set informant to 'none — inferred from household position' with proximity 'researcher', which is correct doctrine per the rubric. Residence correctly uses 'census enumerator' with proximity 'witness' (the enumerator physically visited the dwelling). The primary error is the evidence_type misclassification noted above, not the informant identification itself.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are systematically misclassified. All non-relationship assertions (name, age, birthplace, occupation) for all five people are marked 'direct' when they should be 'indirect'. The rubric doctrine states: 'A fact explicitly stated but reported by an informant who did not witness it is indirect.' On the 1850 census, the informant cannot be identified; no household member can be confirmed as the reporter. An unconfirmed informant reporting facts about themselves or others constitutes secondhand/indirect evidence, not direct. The skill correctly classified relationship inferences as 'indirect' (household position, no explicit relationship column), but failed to apply the same logic to the primary facts. This is a fail-level systematic error affecting the genealogist's ability to trust evidence classifications for downstream conflict resolution.

Your instinct was exactly right. **"Nadnesen" is almost certainly a mistranscription of "Aadnesen"** — this is a well-documented OCR/indexer slip where the leading **"Aa"** of the Norwegian patronymic *Aadnesen* (son of Aadne/Åne) is misread as **"N"**, producing "adnesen" → "Nadnesen."
